questions.question: What is the best first question before picking between relational, key-value, and document storage?
options: Which database brand the team already knows best.
What the primary reads, writes, and invariants actually are.
Whether the product might one day use machine learning.
Which store has the lowest benchmarked single-node latency.
questions.explanation: Storage selection starts with workload shape and correctness requirements, not vendor familiarity.

questions.question: Why is a pure key-value store often a poor fit for invariant-heavy workflows?
options: Because key-value systems cannot replicate data across nodes.
Because they make multi-entity constraints and rich queries harder to enforce directly.
Because they only support string keys and no binary data.
Because they require every client to own its own partition map.
questions.explanation: When the core problem is transactional integrity across entities, simple key lookup is not enough.

questions.question: What is the clearest reason to keep one canonical system of record in a mixed-storage design?
options: It eliminates the need for metrics on derived systems.
It prevents confusion about where correctness lives when indexes or caches lag.
It guarantees every query will be strongly consistent globally.
It allows all schema changes to happen without deployment coordination.
questions.explanation: Without a named source of truth, stale derived systems can silently become accidental authorities.

questions.question: Which workload most strongly points toward relational storage as the primary database?
options: Low-latency per-session lookups with no secondary queries.
A nested profile document that evolves weekly and is fetched as a whole object.
Order placement that must atomically reserve inventory and record payment intent.
An append-only event stream used mostly for analytics backfill.
questions.explanation: Atomic multi-entity updates with business constraints are a classic relational fit.

questions.question: What is the hidden cost of adding many secondary indexes to a flexible document or relational workload?
options: Clients can no longer retry writes safely.
Indexes increase write work, storage cost, and operational tuning pressure.
Primary keys stop being unique under burst traffic.
Replication becomes impossible across regions.
questions.explanation: Every maintained index consumes storage and usually makes writes more expensive.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals that the team is using the wrong storage path for common queries?
options: Latency and volume broken down by lookup, secondary query, and scan class.
Only total disk usage across all database nodes.
Build duration for the application repository.
Average TLS handshake time at the load balancer.
questions.explanation: Per-query-class visibility exposes when a supposedly rare scan becomes the hot path.

questions.question: When is a document model usually more attractive than a tightly normalized relational schema?
options: When object shape evolves quickly and most reads fetch the object as a whole.
When every request needs many transactional joins with strict constraints.
When the main requirement is monthly quota counting by key.
When the system only stores ephemeral session tokens.
questions.explanation: Document storage fits evolving, nested object retrieval patterns better than heavy normalization.

questions.question: What is the senior-level trade-off summary for storage choice in interviews?
options: Choose the most scalable store and add transactions later if needed.
Choose the most familiar store so the rest of the design discussion is faster.
Choose the store that matches the dominant access paths and invariants, then name what it does poorly.
Choose two stores by default to show breadth and architectural maturity.
questions.explanation: Strong answers make the fit explicit and acknowledge the limitations up front.
